paragraphs.text: Strava welcomes the opportunity to respond to the European Commission's Digital Fairness Act call for evidence to highlight how digital platforms can be designed to serve genuine human flourishing. We fully support the Commission's objectives of ensuring a high level of consumer protection within the EU digital single market and addressing the challenges consumers face online.

evidence_quotes.quote: Strava is a subscription-based platform designed to support active lifestyles.

paragraphs.text: Strava is a subscription-based platform designed to support active lifestyles. The platform provides structured training tools, performance insights, route planning and progress tracking to help users set and achieve their personal goals. Features such as training logs, custom goals, segment leaderboards and safety tools are designed to encourage consistent, healthy habits and make activity easier, safer and more rewarding.

evidence_quotes.quote: Strava uses the behavioural science playbook to generate engagement

paragraphs.text: Strava is dedicated to a consumer-centric experience, where user experience (UX) design choices purposefully pursue health and wellbeing first and foremost. Strava uses the behavioural science playbook to generate engagement, attract and retain users. However, our design choices are oriented toward wellbeing and healthy sports habits, rather than endless engagement. By way of example, for every 2 minutes subscribers spend on Strava, they spend ~60 minutes being active.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design

paragraphs.text: As the Commission focuses its attention on addictive design and engagement-driven features as part of its thinking on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act, it is important to recognise the distinction between engagement-driven features employed with the purpose of creating healthy habits that keep users motivated versus engagement-driven features that encourage passive consumption. Our engagement features explicitly motivate users to pursue real-world activity.

evidence_quotes.quote: distinguish between features that promote positive, self-directed behaviours—such as training for a marathon or maintaining a regular exercise routine—and those that encourage excessive or passive use

paragraphs.text: Strava believes that any forthcoming legislation should recognise the purpose and context in which engagement-driven features are deployed. Failing to distinguish between features that promote positive, self-directed behaviours—such as training for a marathon or maintaining a regular exercise routine—and those that encourage excessive or passive use could inadvertently penalise platforms that actively support the Commission's goals of enhancing mental and physical wellbeing.

Strava's approach demonstrates that successful digital businesses can be built on engagement-driven features and behavioral principles that actively promote user wellbeing, community connection, and positive lifestyle change whilst simultaneously protecting consumers.

Strava is a fitness and wellness platform for active people, helping 150+ million users worldwide—and across all 27 EU member states—track activities, reach goals, join communities, and build healthier habits. Launched in 2009 with the mission to inspire more active lives, Strava has grown into a global community where runners, cyclists, swimmers, hikers, and active people of every level celebrate achievements and motivate each other.

Strava's mission aligns with the EU public health priorities: promoting active, healthy lifestyles and combating sedentary behaviour (WHO Europe, 2016–2025). Strava's core features also closely reflect the New EU Workplan for Sport and this Commission's key objectives on wellbeing, health, and physical activity in the pursuit of both mental and physical health. Independent research shows that health-focused apps can significantly increase physical activity and improve clinical outcomes (Nishi et al., 2024) and that apps can support consistent physical activity and healthier lifestyles (Coughlin et al., 2016).

Our platform serves a unique role in the digital ecosystem as it actively encourages people to put down their devices and engage with the physical world. Every interaction on Strava begins with real-world activity: a run through city streets, a bike ride in the countryside, a hike up a mountain, or sweating through a fitness class. Strava's role is to support these moments, not replace them. This foundational design principle shapes everything we do and sets us apart: for every 2 minutes subscribers spend on Strava, around 60 minutes are spent being active offline.
Figure 1: Range of activity types supported on Strava

Community is central to this experience. Today, there are over 1 million clubs on the Strava platform, and group activities receive twice as much engagement as solo ones, a reflection of a positive, supportive, and encouraging environment. Over the past year alone, users have exchanged more than 14 billion kudos (i.e., likes). With 50 activity types supported, Strava offers a space for every kind of mover, from casual walkers to elite competitors.

Long-term health outcomes depend on sustained behaviour change. Decades of behavioural science research show that habits are more likely to form and endure when supported by external prompts, social accountability, and positive reinforcement (Gardner, 2015; Wood, 2019; Singh, 2024).
Strava applies these behavioural science principles through a community-driven design that helps people not only initiate, but maintain, active lifestyles. Core features include:
Goal-setting and progress tracking (Figure 2): providing structure, direction, and a sense of achievement.
Social recognition, clubs, and challenges (Figure 3): fostering accountability and motivation for the next real-world activity.
Segments, achievements, and friendly competition: inspiring participation and progress, not prolonged scrolling.
Figure 2: Strava Profile Page displaying weekly progress across ride activities.

This design encourages real-world activity rather than passive engagement, with many reporting measurable progress toward personal goals, from running further to building consistent weekly routines. Our mid-year data tells us that 41% of Strava subscribers are on pace—or already ahead—of meeting their annual goals. Cyclists lead with an average of 53% progress in their goals, followed by runners at 51%. These outcomes highlight how community engagement, goal-setting, and light-touch gamification can drive sustainable activity habits over time, supporting public health and wellbeing objectives across Europe. (Strava Mid-Year Data Report 2025, Strava Press).

Together, the alignment between research and user outcomes illustrates how Strava leverages digital technology and behavioral science-informed design to make healthy behaviours sustainable, showing how digital platforms can contribute meaningfully to the EU health and wellbeing objectives.
Figure 3: Strava Page displaying community walk

evidence_quotes.quote: intentionally designed to prioritise real-world movement over prolonged digital engagement.

paragraphs.text: Strava's platform is intentionally designed to prioritise real-world movement over prolonged digital engagement. Users must first engage in an activity like running on a trail, cycling along a path, walking their dog, or practicing yoga before they have content to share or engage with. Our features are structured to encourage people to put down the phone and get active: when users see friends completing a new route or achieving a personal best, the response is to save the route and head out.

evidence_quotes.quote: the ability to share location with trusted contacts when you're being active [Figure 4]

paragraphs.text: Our notification system reflects this philosophy. Notifications are centred on activity-related prompts, such as workout reminders, safety features (like the ability to share location with trusted contacts when you're being active [Figure 4]), and meaningful community interactions designed to encourage people in being active offline.
Strava also has natural usage boundaries into the product. Once a workout is finished, the primary interaction with the platform ends, aligning use of the app with real-world activity patterns.
Figure 4: Strava Beacon, safety feature

This principle is embedded across the platform. Strava's Relative Effort feature (Figure 5), for example, analyses training load over time and uses notifications to encourage balance. If someone has completed a particularly intense week, they may receive a prompt to ease off or take a lighter load the following week supporting rest and recovery as essential parts of a sustainable routine. This further reinforces healthy, balanced activity.

Our data shows this design approach works. Increased engagement with Strava correlates directly with increased outdoor activity. Our Year in Sport report outlines that community and social connections are a major driver of this effect; for example, there was a 59% year-over-year increase in global running club participation in 2024, and large group activities increased by 13%. Together, these outcomes demonstrate how Strava encourages real-world activity, foster community, and support sustainable routines.
Figure 5: Strava page showing Relative Effort

From our founding, Strava has prioritised transparency and user autonomy in our platform design. Our value proposition is straightforward: we provide tools and community support to help people stay active and achieve their fitness goals. This translates into clear messaging for users as well, who easily understand what they're signing up for: a platform to track their activities, connect with other active people, and stay motivated in their fitness journeys. Users are empowered to determine their own experience, set their own goals, and engage with the community to their liking.

The vast majority of Strava's core features are offered to users entirely free of charge. These include recording activities, route tracking, connecting with others, participating in challenges, and accessing safety features. This free offering reflects Strava's mission to make movement accessible and enjoyable for everyone, and ensures that users can benefit from the platform without needing to pay for additional services.

evidence_quotes.quote: Our subscription model provides an optional layer of enhanced functionality for those who want more in-depth exercise analytics and performance insights.
no hidden fees or surprise charges.

paragraphs.text: Our subscription model provides an optional layer of enhanced functionality for those who want more in-depth exercise analytics and performance insights. It offers clearly labelled premium features with transparent pricing, straightforward cancellation processes, and no hidden fees or surprise charges.
Figure 6: Showcasing an endemic brand partnership

evidence_quotes.quote: manage their subscriptions (or downgrade)

paragraphs.text: Users can also easily manage their subscriptions (or downgrade) as their needs change.

evidence_quotes.quote: This transparent approach to product design

paragraphs.text: This transparent approach to product design gives users clear choices and control, aligns with EU principles of fairness and transparency, and fosters trust between Strava and its community.

evidence_quotes.quote: monetise personal data through targeted ads.

paragraphs.text: We are not dependent on advertising to sustain our growth, nor do we monetise personal data through targeted ads. While we engage in selective brand partnerships (Figure 6), these are designed to be authentic, relevant, and aligned with our mission, for example, co-hosted challenges or events that motivate users to be more active. These partnerships are not foundational but complementary and optional, with users joining only if they explicitly elect to do so.

evidence_quotes.quote: Users have granular control over activity visibility, choosing who can see their workouts, what information is shared, and how they engage with the broader community.
Our privacy settings (Figure 7) are designed to be intuitive and easily accessible, empowering users to make informed decisions about data sharing preferences.
Users can choose between public and private accounts and adjust privacy settings for each activity.
They also have granular control over what they share, from hiding start and end points to concealing time or entire maps, and can create privacy zones around sensitive locations such as home or work.

paragraphs.text: Our product features, data practices, and platform policies are designed to serve user interests first, with privacy and data control at the centre of our approach. Users have granular control over activity visibility, choosing who can see their workouts, what information is shared, and how they engage with the broader community. Our privacy settings (Figure 7) are designed to be intuitive and easily accessible, empowering users to make informed decisions about data sharing preferences. Users can choose between public and private accounts and adjust privacy settings for each activity. They also have granular control over what they share, from hiding start and end points to concealing time or entire maps, and can create privacy zones around sensitive locations such as home or work.

evidence_quotes.quote: Anyone under 18 is automatically given enhanced default settings: their profiles are private, start and end points are hidden, and direct messaging is disabled for users aged 13-16.

paragraphs.text: Strava is a 13+ platform, and applies stricter safeguards for younger users. Anyone under 18 is automatically given enhanced default settings: their profiles are private, start and end points are hidden, and direct messaging is disabled for users aged 13-16. These measures are designed to protect younger users, who are at a key developmental stage for building lifelong healthy activity habits, while ensuring they can still enjoy the platform in a safe, age-appropriate way. This is reinforced by our community guidelines, which promote positive, supportive interactions that maintain a safe and welcoming environment for users of all backgrounds and abilities.

evidence_quotes.quote: Privacy controls

paragraphs.text: Figure 7: Privacy controls

Strava welcomes the chance to contribute to the Commission's evidence-gathering process on the Digital Fairness Act. This is a significant and timely initiative, and we share the Commission's commitment to safeguarding and empowering consumers in the digital environment.

evidence_quotes.quote: subscriptions

paragraphs.text: Strava's value proposition is straightforward: we provide tools and community support to help people stay active. Users sign up to track their progress and meet their goals. They can easily manage their experience by adjusting notifications, privacy settings, subscriptions, data permissions, and safety features.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative and harmful digital practices

paragraphs.text: Strava recognises the Commission's concerns about manipulative and harmful digital practices, but encourages the Commission to ensure that the upcoming Digital Fairness Act keeps into consideration the fact that not all engagement-drive features pose risks. We believe Strava demonstrates that digital platforms can, with thoughtful design,
innovate along these dimensions while prioritising user wellbeing.

Time spent on Strava translates directly to time spent running, cycling, walking, training, or being active. Unlike platforms optimised for attention retention, Strava motivates users to step away from their devices and into physical activity. Our success is built on delivering value that helps users remain motivated and achieve their personal fitness goals.
Therefore, Strava urges the Commission to:
Distinguish between engagement mechanisms designed to support user goals (e.g., exercising) and those designed for passive use in any upcoming regulation.

Assess engagement features based on intent and impact. Engagement features that demonstrably promote healthy outcomes should not be subject to blanket restrictions. Instead, their design and purpose should be evaluated in context to ensure proportional and evidence-based regulation.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design

paragraphs.text: Strava's story is proof that consumer protection and innovation are not in conflict. Digital platforms can inspire healthier, more connected, and more active lives without manipulative design or exploitative business models.

As the Commission works on its Digital Fairness Act proposal, Strava stands ready to share its experience and evidence, and work with policymakers, regulators, and industry partners to help build a digital ecosystem where platforms serve people and not the other way around.
European Commission Digital Fairness Fitness Check (October 2024)
WHO Europe Physical Activity Strategy for the European Region 2016-2025
